provenance.report: NYC DayQuest — first tranche of 40 hunt ideas

candidates.title: The Cube on One Toe
candidates.neighborhood_category: East Village · public art / playful oddity
candidates.observable_target: The eight-foot black cube balanced on one corner, with visibly uneven faces.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The riddle’s impossible-sounding geometry resolves into a large, unmistakable object.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Public outdoor plaza; view without touching or entering the roadway. Crowds and skaters may require patience; step-free approach.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; permanent sculpture, though temporary maintenance barriers are possible.

source.excerpt: “It is a black cube, 8 feet (2.4 m) long on each side, mounted on a corner.” Also: “The faces of the cube are not flat but have various indentations, protrusions, and ledges.”

evidence.claim: The eight-foot black cube balanced on one corner, with visibly uneven faces.
evidence.viewpoint: Public outdoor plaza; view without touching or entering the roadway. Crowds and skaters may require patience; step-free approach.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; permanent sculpture, though temporary maintenance barriers are possible.

idea.concept: The eight-foot black cube balanced on one corner, with visibly uneven faces. The riddle’s impossible-sounding geometry resolves into a large, unmistakable object.

clue_package.riddle: I weigh nearly a ton, yet balance on one toe. My six dark faces refuse to lie flat. Find me.
hints: Look for geometry where the Village meets Astor Place.
You need the large black cube mounted on a corner.

candidates.title: A Clock That Refuses to Read Normally
candidates.neighborhood_category: Union Square · public art / urban timekeeping
candidates.observable_target: The orange LED clock paired with the concentric brick-wave wall and circular void.
candidates.satisfying_reason: Players first see an apparently indecipherable number, then recognize an artwork about time.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: View from public sidewalk/plaza; heavy pedestrian and bicycle traffic. No need to cross outside signals.
candidates.durability_risk: **Medium**; clock/display programming can change. Do not base the answer on steam, which the source describes only in the past tense.

source.excerpt: the work includes “a round circular void … and a clock made of large orange LED digits”; its brick wall forms “a wave pattern like ripples on still water.”

evidence.claim: The orange LED clock paired with the concentric brick-wave wall and circular void.
evidence.viewpoint: View from public sidewalk/plaza; heavy pedestrian and bicycle traffic. No need to cross outside signals.
evidence.seasonality: **Medium**; clock/display programming can change. Do not base the answer on steam, which the source describes only in the past tense.

idea.concept: The orange LED clock paired with the concentric brick-wave wall and circular void. Players first see an apparently indecipherable number, then recognize an artwork about time.

clue_package.riddle: My orange numbers race in two directions while a stone ripple waits beside them. What time am I trying to tell?
hints: Search the south side of Union Square, above eye level.
Find the orange LED digits beside a circular void in a rippling brick wall.

candidates.title: Manhattan Before Manhattan
candidates.observable_target: The small rectangular plot of native vegetation representing stages of forest growth.
candidates.satisfying_reason: An apparently untended patch becomes a living time machine amid hard-edged city blocks.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Observe from the surrounding public sidewalk; do not enter or disturb vegetation. Sidewalk viewing is generally step-free.
candidates.durability_risk: **Medium-high**; seasonal appearance, pruning, plant loss, and restoration can obscure the three-stage idea.

source.excerpt: it “consists of plants that were native to the New York City area in pre-colonial times”; NYC Parks describes “three stages of forest growth from grasses to saplings to grown trees.”

evidence.claim: The small rectangular plot of native vegetation representing stages of forest growth.
evidence.viewpoint: Observe from the surrounding public sidewalk; do not enter or disturb vegetation. Sidewalk viewing is generally step-free.
evidence.seasonality: **Medium-high**; seasonal appearance, pruning, plant loss, and restoration can obscure the three-stage idea.

idea.concept: The small rectangular plot of native vegetation representing stages of forest growth. An apparently untended patch becomes a living time machine amid hard-edged city blocks.

clue_package.riddle: No cabin, no road, no skyline: I rehearse this island before the city arrived, one forest age at a time.
hints: Look for an artwork that grows rather than hangs.
Find the native-plant plot at University Village.

candidates.title: Six Stories, No Rooms
candidates.observable_target: Knox Martin’s six-story wall painting.
candidates.satisfying_reason: “Six stories” misdirects toward a building interior before resolving into the scale of a painted wall.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Exterior sidewalk view; choose a safe angle without stepping into traffic or blocking entrances.
candidates.durability_risk: **Medium**; murals can be obscured, repainted, scaffolded, or partly hidden by construction.

source.excerpt: “Woman with Bicycle is a six-story wall painting created by … Knox Martin in 1979.”

evidence.claim: Knox Martin’s six-story wall painting.
evidence.viewpoint: Exterior sidewalk view; choose a safe angle without stepping into traffic or blocking entrances.
evidence.seasonality: **Medium**; murals can be obscured, repainted, scaffolded, or partly hidden by construction.

idea.concept: Knox Martin’s six-story wall painting. “Six stories” misdirects toward a building interior before resolving into the scale of a painted wall.

clue_package.riddle: I rise six stories but contain no rooms. A woman and her wheels travel without moving.
hints: Your answer is painted, not built.
Look for Knox Martin’s towering wall work.

candidates.title: The Elder at the Park’s Corner
candidates.observable_target: The mature English elm in the northwest portion of Washington Square Park.
candidates.satisfying_reason: Players must distinguish one living landmark from an entire canopy.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Public park during posted hours; remain on paths and outside any tree-protection fencing. Generally step-free.
candidates.durability_risk: **Medium**; living trees can be pruned, fenced, storm-damaged, or eventually lost.

source.excerpt: “Hangman’s Elm … is an English elm located at the northwest corner in Washington Square Park”; NYC Parks revised its age to “more than 300 years old.”

evidence.claim: The mature English elm in the northwest portion of Washington Square Park.
evidence.viewpoint: Public park during posted hours; remain on paths and outside any tree-protection fencing. Generally step-free.
evidence.seasonality: **Medium**; living trees can be pruned, fenced, storm-damaged, or eventually lost.

idea.concept: The mature English elm in the northwest portion of Washington Square Park. Players must distinguish one living landmark from an entire canopy.

clue_package.riddle: Stone arches arrived after me, and generations passed beneath my limbs. Find Manhattan’s elder Englishman.
hints: The answer is alive and rooted in Washington Square.
Seek the English elm in the park’s northwest corner.

candidates.title: Marble at the End of the Avenue
candidates.observable_target: The white marble triumphal arch terminating Fifth Avenue.
candidates.satisfying_reason: Direction, material, and Roman form converge on one strong silhouette.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Public park paths; do not climb the monument. Step-free views available, with crowd congestion likely.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; permanent landmark, with occasional restoration/scaffolding.

source.excerpt: “The Washington Square Arch … is a marble memorial arch”; it “forms the southern terminus of Fifth Avenue.”

evidence.claim: The white marble triumphal arch terminating Fifth Avenue.
evidence.viewpoint: Public park paths; do not climb the monument. Step-free views available, with crowd congestion likely.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; permanent landmark, with occasional restoration/scaffolding.

idea.concept: The white marble triumphal arch terminating Fifth Avenue. Direction, material, and Roman form converge on one strong silhouette.

clue_package.riddle: An avenue ends where Rome appears in white stone, built for a president who never walked beneath it.
hints: Follow Fifth Avenue south to its ceremonial full stop.
Find Stanford White’s marble arch in Washington Square.

candidates.title: Judgment by Shakespeare
candidates.observable_target: The courthouse-like clock tower and tympanum depicting a scene from *The Merchant of Venice*.
candidates.satisfying_reason: A church-shaped building offers a secular “judgment” scene hidden in its ornament.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Exterior target is sidewalk-visible. Use the library only during posted hours; entrance accessibility should be field-checked.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; carved architectural detail, though foliage or scaffolding may affect visibility.

source.excerpt: the architect added “church-like touches with non-religious content,” including “the tympanum which shows a scene from The Merchant of Venice.”

evidence.claim: The courthouse-like clock tower and tympanum depicting a scene from *The Merchant of Venice*.
evidence.viewpoint: Exterior target is sidewalk-visible. Use the library only during posted hours; entrance accessibility should be field-checked.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; carved architectural detail, though foliage or scaffolding may affect visibility.

idea.concept: The courthouse-like clock tower and tympanum depicting a scene from *The Merchant of Venice*. A church-shaped building offers a secular “judgment” scene hidden in its ornament.

clue_package.riddle: I wear a church’s tower, but over my door Shakespeare—not scripture—sits in judgment.
hints: Look at the former courthouse now filled with books.
Inspect the tympanum for a scene from *The Merchant of Venice*.

candidates.title: A Street Behind a Gate
candidates.observable_target: The gated cul-de-sac and its ten three-story brick row houses.
candidates.satisfying_reason: A whole street hides behind what can look like a private doorway.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: **View only from the public sidewalk. Do not enter the gated lane or disturb residents.** Visibility and curb cuts require field checking.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low-medium**; buildings endure, but gate closure, foliage, or resident privacy measures can block sightlines.

source.excerpt: “Patchin Place is a gated cul-de-sac”; it contains “ten 3-story brick row houses.”

evidence.claim: The gated cul-de-sac and its ten three-story brick row houses.
evidence.viewpoint: **View only from the public sidewalk. Do not enter the gated lane or disturb residents.** Visibility and curb cuts require field checking.
evidence.seasonality: **Low-medium**; buildings endure, but gate closure, foliage, or resident privacy measures can block sightlines.

idea.concept: The gated cul-de-sac and its ten three-story brick row houses. A whole street hides behind what can look like a private doorway.

clue_package.riddle: Ten brick siblings share a street that goes nowhere. Their smallest traffic jam is a locked gate.
hints: Look for a cul-de-sac tucked off a Village cross street.
Find the gate through which ten three-story brick row houses are visible.

candidates.title: Where the Horses Left
candidates.neighborhood_category: Greenwich Village · adaptive reuse / hidden street
candidates.observable_target: Former stable buildings along the private gated mews.
candidates.satisfying_reason: Players identify the equestrian origin of a lane now embedded in NYU’s urban campus.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Observe only from public space unless signage explicitly permits entry; never tailgate through a private gate. Sidewalk view may be partially obstructed.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low-medium**; lane and buildings are durable, but public visibility/access rules can change.

source.excerpt: “Washington Mews is a private gated street”; it “was first developed as a mews (row of stables).”

evidence.claim: Former stable buildings along the private gated mews.
evidence.viewpoint: Observe only from public space unless signage explicitly permits entry; never tailgate through a private gate. Sidewalk view may be partially obstructed.
evidence.seasonality: **Low-medium**; lane and buildings are durable, but public visibility/access rules can change.

idea.concept: Former stable buildings along the private gated mews. Players identify the equestrian origin of a lane now embedded in NYU’s urban campus.

clue_package.riddle: The horses moved out, the scholars moved in, and the little street kept its stable name.
hints: A mews was built to serve horses.
Look through the gate just north of Washington Square.

candidates.title: The Court Called a Cage
candidates.observable_target: The unusually compact basketball court enclosed by fencing.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The nickname is readable in the court’s physical proportions rather than on a plaque.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Public recreation site; do not interrupt games or photograph identifiable players without consent. View from outside fence; nearby sidewalks are step-free.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; court identity is durable, though resurfacing and events can limit access.

source.excerpt: the courts are “also known as ‘The Cage’” and are “noted for [their] non-regulation size.”

evidence.claim: The unusually compact basketball court enclosed by fencing.
evidence.viewpoint: Public recreation site; do not interrupt games or photograph identifiable players without consent. View from outside fence; nearby sidewalks are step-free.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; court identity is durable, though resurfacing and events can limit access.

idea.concept: The unusually compact basketball court enclosed by fencing. The nickname is readable in the court’s physical proportions rather than on a plaque.

clue_package.riddle: My hoops are famous, my boundaries are cramped, and my nickname belongs to something built to keep you in.
hints: Listen for bouncing beneath a web of Village fencing.
Find the non-regulation streetball courts known as The Cage.

candidates.title: The Brass Ancestor of Cappuccino
candidates.neighborhood_category: Greenwich Village · food culture / mechanical artifact
candidates.observable_target: The 1902 espresso machine against the café’s back wall.
candidates.satisfying_reason: An ornate machine becomes a café treasure hunt with a precise date and location.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Private business during posted hours; patronize respectfully, ask before photography, and never obstruct staff. Interior route and restroom accessibility require field verification.
candidates.durability_risk: **Medium-low**; artifact is longstanding but interior placement, hours, or photo policy may change.

source.excerpt: “Inside the cafe, against the back wall, is the original espresso machine, made in 1902.”

evidence.claim: The 1902 espresso machine against the café’s back wall.
evidence.viewpoint: Private business during posted hours; patronize respectfully, ask before photography, and never obstruct staff. Interior route and restroom accessibility require field verification.
evidence.seasonality: **Medium-low**; artifact is longstanding but interior placement, hours, or photo policy may change.

idea.concept: The 1902 espresso machine against the café’s back wall. An ornate machine becomes a café treasure hunt with a precise date and location.

clue_package.riddle: Long before countertop pods, this brass elder helped teach America to drink a cloud of milk over coffee.
hints: Your answer is inside a longstanding MacDougal Street café.
Look against the back wall for the espresso machine made in 1902.

candidates.title: The Pink Palazzo
candidates.observable_target: The bold pink Venetian-palazzo-style building above a former stable.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The building is deliberately theatrical and radically unlike its neighbors.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Exterior-only sidewalk observation; respect residential privacy and do not photograph into windows.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; distinctive permanent architecture, though repainting/scaffolding is possible.

source.excerpt: the building is designed “in the style of a Venetian palazzo” and “is easy to spot because of its singular style and bold pink color.”

evidence.claim: The bold pink Venetian-palazzo-style building above a former stable.
evidence.viewpoint: Exterior-only sidewalk observation; respect residential privacy and do not photograph into windows.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; distinctive permanent architecture, though repainting/scaffolding is possible.

idea.concept: The bold pink Venetian-palazzo-style building above a former stable. The building is deliberately theatrical and radically unlike its neighbors.

clue_package.riddle: Venice sailed to the Village, landed on a horse stable, and blushed bright pink.
hints: Look for a palazzo where no canal runs.
Find Julian Schnabel’s bold pink building.

candidates.title: Half an Address, Half a House
candidates.observable_target: The exceptionally narrow townhouse and its half-number address.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The fractional address becomes a fair clue to the building’s squeezed dimensions.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Public sidewalk only; private residence, so avoid windows, doorways, and lingering crowds.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; landmark-district building, although facade work can obscure it.

source.excerpt: the house “is only 9 feet 6 inches (2.9 meters) wide” and is “often described as the narrowest house in New York.”

evidence.claim: The exceptionally narrow townhouse and its half-number address.
evidence.viewpoint: Public sidewalk only; private residence, so avoid windows, doorways, and lingering crowds.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; landmark-district building, although facade work can obscure it.

idea.concept: The exceptionally narrow townhouse and its half-number address. The fractional address becomes a fair clue to the building’s squeezed dimensions.

clue_package.riddle: My address needed half a number because my house received barely a whole share of the street.
hints: Search Bedford Street for a fraction.
Find the townhouse documented as only 9 feet 6 inches wide.

candidates.title: Houses Stacked in the Sky
candidates.observable_target: The tower’s irregular, cantilevered stack of floors—the “Jenga” silhouette.
candidates.satisfying_reason: A tabletop game metaphor becomes obvious only when players look up.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: View from a sufficiently wide public sidewalk; watch cyclists and avoid leaning into the street.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; permanent skyline form, with temporary construction obstructions possible.

source.excerpt: the “Jenga Building” was described by Herzog & de Meuron as “houses stacked in the sky.”

evidence.claim: The tower’s irregular, cantilevered stack of floors—the “Jenga” silhouette.
evidence.viewpoint: View from a sufficiently wide public sidewalk; watch cyclists and avoid leaning into the street.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; permanent skyline form, with temporary construction obstructions possible.

idea.concept: The tower’s irregular, cantilevered stack of floors—the “Jenga” silhouette. A tabletop game metaphor becomes obvious only when players look up.

clue_package.riddle: A giant began a game of blocks, then walked away before the tower could fall.
hints: Look above Tribeca for deliberately uneven stacking.
Find the skyscraper nicknamed the Jenga Building.

candidates.title: The Skyscraper That Cannot Look Out
candidates.observable_target: The tall, largely windowless telecommunications building.
candidates.satisfying_reason: A familiar skyscraper expectation—thousands of windows—is completely inverted.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Exterior observation from public sidewalk only. It is critical infrastructure: no entry, tailgating, equipment photography, or security interference.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; major infrastructure building.

source.excerpt: “33 Thomas Street … is a 550-foot-tall … windowless skyscraper” and a telephone exchange/wire center.

evidence.claim: The tall, largely windowless telecommunications building.
evidence.viewpoint: Exterior observation from public sidewalk only. It is critical infrastructure: no entry, tailgating, equipment photography, or security interference.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; major infrastructure building.

idea.concept: The tall, largely windowless telecommunications building. A familiar skyscraper expectation—thousands of windows—is completely inverted.

clue_package.riddle: I stand taller than many buildings that watch the city, yet I have almost no eyes.
hints: Search for telecommunications architecture, not apartments.
Find Tribeca’s 550-foot windowless skyscraper.

candidates.title: A Gothic Giant in Terracotta
candidates.observable_target: The Neo-Gothic tower whose exterior is largely architectural terracotta over a limestone base.
candidates.satisfying_reason: What reads as carved stone at skyline scale is revealed as a different material.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Exterior clue; use park/sidewalk viewpoints rather than stopping in crosswalks. Lobby access is not required.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; landmark facade, subject to periodic restoration netting.

source.excerpt: “Its facade is mostly clad with architectural terracotta, though the lower portions are limestone.”

evidence.claim: The Neo-Gothic tower whose exterior is largely architectural terracotta over a limestone base.
evidence.viewpoint: Exterior clue; use park/sidewalk viewpoints rather than stopping in crosswalks. Lobby access is not required.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; landmark facade, subject to periodic restoration netting.

idea.concept: The Neo-Gothic tower whose exterior is largely architectural terracotta over a limestone base. What reads as carved stone at skyline scale is revealed as a different material.

clue_package.riddle: I dressed a modern steel giant like a Gothic cathedral, mostly in fired earth instead of carved stone.
hints: Look toward the early skyscraper once tallest in the world.
Inspect the Woolworth Building’s terracotta facade above its limestone base.

candidates.title: Fame Above City Hall
candidates.observable_target: The gilded *Civic Fame* statue at the building’s summit.
candidates.satisfying_reason: Players must find a small golden figure at enormous scale rather than another street-level monument.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Observe from public plaza or sidewalk; do not stand in bridge or vehicle approaches. Step-free viewpoints exist but must be field-selected.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; permanent statue, though distance, glare, fog, and scaffolding affect playability.

source.excerpt: “The base incorporates a subway station, while the top includes the gilded Civic Fame statue.”

evidence.claim: The gilded *Civic Fame* statue at the building’s summit.
evidence.viewpoint: Observe from public plaza or sidewalk; do not stand in bridge or vehicle approaches. Step-free viewpoints exist but must be field-selected.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; permanent statue, though distance, glare, fog, and scaffolding affect playability.

idea.concept: The gilded *Civic Fame* statue at the building’s summit. Players must find a small golden figure at enormous scale rather than another street-level monument.

clue_package.riddle: Fame stands on the city’s shoulders, dressed in gold and too high to hear applause.
hints: Look above a building whose base contains a subway station.
Find the gilded figure named *Civic Fame*.

candidates.title: A Piece of Ireland on a Manhattan Roof
candidates.observable_target: Irish stones, native vegetation, and the reconstructed cottage integrated into the sloping memorial.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The reveal is a transported landscape rather than a conventional statue or plaque.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Public site subject to posted hours/closures. Sloped and uneven surfaces may challenge some users; provide a ground-level alternative viewpoint after field audit.
candidates.durability_risk: **Medium**; vegetation is seasonal and weather/maintenance can close the upper path.

source.excerpt: it is landscaped with “stones, soil, and native vegetation transported from the western coast of Ireland—with stones from every Irish county”; the site incorporates “an authentic Irish cottage.”

evidence.claim: Irish stones, native vegetation, and the reconstructed cottage integrated into the sloping memorial.
evidence.viewpoint: Public site subject to posted hours/closures. Sloped and uneven surfaces may challenge some users; provide a ground-level alternative viewpoint after field audit.
evidence.seasonality: **Medium**; vegetation is seasonal and weather/maintenance can close the upper path.

idea.concept: Irish stones, native vegetation, and the reconstructed cottage integrated into the sloping memorial. The reveal is a transported landscape rather than a conventional statue or plaque.

clue_package.riddle: Every county sent a stone, the western coast sent its green, and an abandoned cottage crossed an ocean.
hints: Look for a memorial designed as terrain.
Find the Irish cottage among soil, stones, and native vegetation.

candidates.title: A Loire Château for Fire Engines
candidates.observable_target: The former firehouse styled after an early-sixteenth-century Loire Valley château.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The building type and its aristocratic visual costume are charmingly mismatched.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Exterior sidewalk view; do not block doors or enter the current community facility without permission.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; landmark architecture.

source.excerpt: architects Napoleon LeBrun & Sons “styled it after early-16th-century chateaux in the Loire Valley of France.”

evidence.claim: The former firehouse styled after an early-sixteenth-century Loire Valley château.
evidence.viewpoint: Exterior sidewalk view; do not block doors or enter the current community facility without permission.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; landmark architecture.

idea.concept: The former firehouse styled after an early-sixteenth-century Loire Valley château. The building type and its aristocratic visual costume are charmingly mismatched.

clue_package.riddle: My doors once welcomed engines, but my architects dressed me for a French noble beside the Loire.
hints: This château was built for firefighters, not royalty.
Find former Engine Company 31.

candidates.title: The Street That Changes Its Mind
candidates.observable_target: The one-block street’s sharp central bend.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The answer is a street’s physical shape, not a building or sign.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Remain on sidewalk/pedestrianized areas and respect businesses. Pavement, curb cuts, deliveries, and crowding require field audit.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low** for geometry; **medium** for paving/paint treatments.

source.excerpt: “It is one block long with a sharp bend in the middle.”

evidence.claim: The one-block street’s sharp central bend.
evidence.viewpoint: Remain on sidewalk/pedestrianized areas and respect businesses. Pavement, curb cuts, deliveries, and crowding require field audit.
evidence.seasonality: **Low** for geometry; **medium** for paving/paint treatments.

idea.concept: The one-block street’s sharp central bend. The answer is a street’s physical shape, not a building or sign.

clue_package.riddle: I am only one block long, yet halfway through I abruptly change my mind.
hints: Follow a Chinatown lane too short for a gentle curve.
Find the sharp bend in Doyers Street.

candidates.title: A Rose Between Two Towers
candidates.canonical_place: Eldridge Street Synagogue / Museum at Eldridge Street
candidates.observable_target: The facade’s rose window framed by two stair towers and arched openings.
candidates.satisfying_reason: “Rose” is a fair visual metaphor whose architectural meaning becomes clear at arrival.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Facade is public-sidewalk view. Interior museum admission/hours must not be required; avoid photographing worshippers.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; restored facade, with possible event or restoration obstructions.

source.excerpt: “The Eldridge Street facade includes a rose window, two stair towers, and arched openings.”

evidence.claim: The facade’s rose window framed by two stair towers and arched openings.
evidence.viewpoint: Facade is public-sidewalk view. Interior museum admission/hours must not be required; avoid photographing worshippers.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; restored facade, with possible event or restoration obstructions.

idea.concept: The facade’s rose window framed by two stair towers and arched openings. “Rose” is a fair visual metaphor whose architectural meaning becomes clear at arrival.

clue_package.riddle: A glass rose blooms between twin stair towers, watered only by city light.
hints: The flower is circular architecture, not a plant.
Find the rose window on the Eldridge Street facade.

candidates.title: The Bank’s Returned Crown
candidates.observable_target: The reconstructed rooftop tempietto and dome.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The target is a classical “little temple” that disappeared and later returned.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Exterior view only. Select a safe sidewalk sightline; no private hotel access is needed.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low** structurally; visibility depends on angle, weather, and nearby construction.

source.excerpt: “In 2014, the New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission approved a replica tempietto, which was completed by the beginning of 2020.”

evidence.claim: The reconstructed rooftop tempietto and dome.
evidence.viewpoint: Exterior view only. Select a safe sidewalk sightline; no private hotel access is needed.
evidence.seasonality: **Low** structurally; visibility depends on angle, weather, and nearby construction.

idea.concept: The reconstructed rooftop tempietto and dome. The target is a classical “little temple” that disappeared and later returned.

clue_package.riddle: A tiny temple lost its perch for thirty years, then climbed back onto a banker’s roof.
hints: Look above a former Lower East Side bank.
Find the replica tempietto completed around 2020.

candidates.title: The Odd Roof Out
candidates.observable_target: The later mansard roof sitting above the earlier Italianate building.
candidates.satisfying_reason: Players identify an architectural “hat” added decades after the body beneath it.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Public sidewalk view; crossing may improve sightline but must use signals.
candidates.durability_risk: **Low**; durable roof form, occasionally obscured by scaffolding.

source.excerpt: “The mansard roof was an addition … built in 1881–1882”; the original building was Italianate.

evidence.claim: The later mansard roof sitting above the earlier Italianate building.
evidence.viewpoint: Public sidewalk view; crossing may improve sightline but must use signals.
evidence.seasonality: **Low**; durable roof form, occasionally obscured by scaffolding.

idea.concept: The later mansard roof sitting above the earlier Italianate building. Players identify an architectural “hat” added decades after the body beneath it.

clue_package.riddle: I was born Italianate, then decades later received a steep French hat.
hints: The answer’s hat is younger than the building.
Find the mansard roof added to Odd Fellows Hall.

candidates.title: The Memorial at Eight Roads
candidates.canonical_place: Kimlau War Memorial at Chatham Square
candidates.observable_target: The Kimlau memorial arch amid Chatham Square’s unusually complex street convergence.
candidates.satisfying_reason: The calm stone arch contrasts with a knot of moving streets.
candidates.access_safety_mobility: Busy, multi-leg intersection. Use only marked crossings and an accessible plaza approach; construction is a significant possibility.
candidates.durability_risk: **Medium-high**; a major Kimlau Square redesign has been announced, so layout and access must be checked immediately before publication.

source.excerpt: Chatham Square lies at “the confluence of eight streets”; “The Kimlau War Memorial was erected … to honor United States service members of Chinese ancestry.”

evidence.claim: The Kimlau memorial arch amid Chatham Square’s unusually complex street convergence.
evidence.viewpoint: Busy, multi-leg intersection. Use only marked crossings and an accessible plaza approach; construction is a significant possibility.

rejected_candidates.reason: The committed source explicitly says the garden “was demolished on December 31, 1997”; unsuitable as a present-day observable target.
Source says steam was emitted “at one point”; the orange clock and brick-wave wall remain the defensible target.
Available source confirmed the building and historic-district context but did not provide sufficiently exact quoted evidence for the proposed color/detail.
Residential public-housing context creates privacy and ambiguous-public-access concerns; the online source did not establish a clearly public, distinctive target.
The business and menu are sourced, but current colors, lettering, and awning configuration were not established; entry 40 avoids inventing them.
Encourages touching an overcrowded sculpture and relies on a behavior-driven, potentially inappropriate detail.
It is explicitly a private gated street; only a lawful public-sidewalk view is acceptable.
Commonly described in secondary guides, but the source reviewed here did not provide strong enough evidence of current presence/visibility.
The original was destroyed; the current crown must be described accurately as a replica completed around 2020.
Street geometry is durable; paint color/design is subject to resurfacing and was not sufficiently established by the reviewed source.
Rotating panels are observable, but players should not be instructed to move them without staff invitation.
Sourced as an interior object, but provenance and current public visibility merit stronger editorial and field confirmation before use.
